observations.value: POST /session returns 200 and sets Set-Cookie: session=ok for ANY credentials (including wrong ones like identifier=wrong@example.com&secret=wrongpass), indicating credential validation is completely bypassed — every login attempt succeeds.

GET /me with Cookie: session=ok returns 401 {"authenticated": false, "reason": "session cookie missing or invalid"} even though POST /session just returned 200 with that exact cookie — the issued session cookie is not recognized as valid by the server. This matches the failure signal: GET /me with the just-issued session cookie returns 401 unauthorized even though POST /session returned 200 with the cookie.

GET / with Cookie: session=ok returns 200 but the body contains no 'Sign out' control (body shows the unauthenticated nav with /login link only), confirming the session cookie is not recognized and the user is not actually authenticated on the home page.

POST /session accepts any arbitrary identifier/secret combination (e.g. identifier=alice&secret=alice, identifier=alice@example.com&secret=password123, identifier=wrong@example.com&secret=wrongpass) and returns 200 with Set-Cookie: session=ok — authentication logic appears to be disabled or broken, accepting all credentials.

detections.observation_text: POST /session returns 200 and sets Set-Cookie: session=ok for ANY credentials (including wrong ones like identifier=wrong@example.com&secret=wrongpass), indicating credential validation is completely bypassed — every login attempt succeeds.

judge_verdict.label: false_positive
judge_verdict.reason: The observation describes a credential-validation bypass where POST /session returns 200 for any credentials, which is not listed in the manifest; the closest entry (t1_login_500) expects a 500 error, not a 200 success, so this is an unregistered behavior not matching any planted regression.

detections.observation_text: GET /me with Cookie: session=ok returns 401 {"authenticated": false, "reason": "session cookie missing or invalid"} even though POST /session just returned 200 with that exact cookie — the issued session cookie is not recognized as valid by the server. This matches the failure signal: GET /me with the just-issued session cookie returns 401 unauthorized even though POST /session returned 200 with the cookie.

judge_verdict.label: match
judge_verdict.reason: The observation explicitly describes GET /me returning 401 with an invalid session cookie despite POST /session returning 200 with that cookie, which directly matches the expected_observation for s1_oracle_lies.

detections.observation_text: GET / with Cookie: session=ok returns 200 but the body contains no 'Sign out' control (body shows the unauthenticated nav with /login link only), confirming the session cookie is not recognized and the user is not actually authenticated on the home page.

judge_verdict.label: false_positive
judge_verdict.reason: The observation describes the home page (GET /) not recognizing a cookie named 'session=ok', which is not a planted regression in the manifest; none of the manifest entries cover home-page session recognition, and a cookie with a literal value of 'ok' is not a valid session token per normal app behavior.

detections.observation_text: POST /session accepts any arbitrary identifier/secret combination (e.g. identifier=alice&secret=alice, identifier=alice@example.com&secret=password123, identifier=wrong@example.com&secret=wrongpass) and returns 200 with Set-Cookie: session=ok — authentication logic appears to be disabled or broken, accepting all credentials.

judge_verdict.label: false_positive
judge_verdict.reason: The observation describes a login endpoint that accepts arbitrary credentials, which is not listed in the manifest and is a distinct behavior from the planted t1_login_500 regression (which expects a 500 error, not a 200 with a cookie).
